Evolve
I used to be a junior developer at IBM.
Every night I would stay late to check the servers.
The landscape used to be much slower than it is today.
We would guard our computer time like gold.
Lucia, your resume spans four decades, from IBM in the eighties all the way to Google today. Take me back to the beginning. What did a normal day use to look like for you at IBM?
Back when I was at IBM, I used to manage entire data center migrations by hand. Every night I would stay late, checking each server one by one. That whole landscape is obsolete now.
That is a completely different world. So what changed? What was the biggest shift you lived through?
The move to the cloud transformed everything. I used to believe that on-premise was safer, but the industry evolved, and I evolved with it. Today I lead in a completely new era.
I love that. When you reflect on those forty-one years, what does that long journey give you that a younger candidate does not have?
Perspective. I have watched three technologies become obsolete. So when a new one arrives, I do not panic. I used to fear change; now I look for it.
That is exactly what we need on the AI safety team. Last one: how would you say a senior leader's job today is different from what it use to be?
We used to give orders and expect people to follow. Now the job is to build trust and to reflect out loud, so the team learns how you think, not just what you decide.
Back when I was at IBM, I used to manage everything by hand.
Every morning I would arrive early and check each system.
The whole landscape has transformed since I started.
I used to fear change; now I look for it.
When I reflect on forty-one years, I see how much has evolved.

Let me tell you what this industry used to be like, because most people today have no idea. When I started, back in the mainframe era, we used to book computer time in advance. You would get a two-hour slot at three in the morning, and you would guard it like gold. There was no cloud, no laptops. A single machine used to fill an entire room. Every company used to run its own data center, and if a server failed, someone would drive in at midnight to fix it by hand. That whole landscape is obsolete now. In one decade, everything transformed. What used to take a team of thirty now takes one engineer and a single command. People ask me if I miss the old days. Honestly, no. But I would not trade the perspective. When you have watched the ground shift three times, you stop fearing the next change, and you start looking for it.

So they asked me how the job has changed since I started in nineteen eighty-three. Here is what I said. Back then, a senior technology leader used to be the person with all the answers. You would sit at the top, you would give the orders, and the team would execute. Technology used to move slowly, so a single decision could last for years. That era is gone. Today the landscape shifts every few months, and no leader can know everything. So the job has transformed. I used to manage systems; now I build trust. I used to protect the old way; now I help my team evolve with the change. When I reflect on forty-one years, that is the biggest shift of all. It is not in the technology, but in what leadership means. The best leaders today do not pretend to be certain. They stay curious, and they keep learning out loud.

When I started in the mainframe era, I used to manage everything by hand. Every night, I would stay late to check each server one by one. Then the shift to the cloud transformed the whole landscape. The systems I used to run are completely obsolete now. When I reflect on it, I evolved with every decade.
